data.title: Vera Therapeutics: Huge Catalyst Upcoming For Kidney Disease Drug
data.description:
Investment Overview—Understanding My “Hold” Call In January 2024

The last time I reviewed Vera Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VERA), it was January 2024, and I gave the company's stock a “Hold” recommendation, with shares trading at a value of $25. In that note, I wrote:

Shareholders will be celebrating today, as the Brisbane, California-based company shared positive 72-week data from the open-label extension (OLE) period of its Phase 2b ORIGIN clinical trial of its lead candidate atacicept in participants with IgA nephropathy (IgAN).

At the time of writing, Vera's share price was up >50% for the day, trading at a value of ~$26 per share, which values the company at around $1bn. Vera stock is now up >160% on a 1-year basis, and shares trade at their highest value since late 2021.

I noted, however, that at the time >20% of Vera's share float was held short, and concluded:

Ultimately, due to the fact that Vera has no other assets of note and the length of time it will take to gather the pivotal data for an approval push, I would give the stock a "hold" rating for now. Kidney disease is a tricky indication to target, but it seems as though, since I last covered Vera back in Jan 2022, although the company has made some admirable progress, other companies have too.

In this post, I'll cover what has happened since January last year, and what, I think, is going to happen with atrasentan, and how that will likely affect the stock price going forward.

Updates Since Last January—Pivotal Data Very Close To Being Shared, But Otsuka Upsets The Apple Cart

Initially, my hold call looked wide of the mark as the bull run continued—by the end of February 2024, the stock traded at $49 per share, up nearly 100%. As a reminder, Vera IPO'd in May 2021, raising ~$48m via the issuance of 4.35m shares, priced at $11 per share.

According to Vera's Q1 2025 quarterly report / 10Q submission:

We completed full enrollment of ORIGIN 3, the pivotal Phase 3 trial of atacicept 150 mg in IgAN, in April 2025, and in September 2024 completed enrollment of the initial cohort of 200 participants that will provide data for the 36-week urine protein creatinine ratio (UPCR) primary efficacy endpoint. We expect to announce primary endpoint results in the second quarter of 2025, supporting our planned submission for regulatory approval later in 2025.

Below is an overview of the Phase 3 study as shared in Vera's latest investor presentation:

As we can see, the primary endpoint is UPCR-24h—change in proteinuria as evaluated by urine protein creatinine ratio—at week 36.

In my last note, I mentioned that Vera faces some significant competitors in the IgaN market—yet Vera insists its drug is "best-in-class", as shown in the slide below:

Analysts at Goldman Sachs (GS) upgraded Vera stock in January this year, calling atacicept "largely de-risked, with a line of sight to commercialization starting next year." However, in February Vera's share price began to tumble as Otsuka (OTCPK:OTSKF) updated on sibeprenlimab, leading analysts to conclude that, not only could the drug reach the market months ahead of atacicept, its less frequent dosing regime could hand it a crucial edge in commercial markets—although we may note atacicept can be self-injected at home, while the other therapies, including sibeprenlimab, must be injected at a clinic.

As such, at the time of writing, Vera's stock price has fallen to $21 per share, down >15% since my last note, despite, at one stage, being up >100%.

The slide above shows us that Vera, Otsuka, and Vertex (VRTX) and Novartis' (NVS) drugs have similar mechanisms of action (MoAs), targeting either APRIL, or both BAFF and APRIL. Here is an explanation of why these proteins are key targets for drug developers, taken from the frontiers website:

The “four-hit hypothesis” of IgAN pathogenesis outlines a process that begins with elevated circulating levels of Gd-IgA1 that trigger autoantibody production. This results in the formation and deposition of immune complexes in the mesangium, leading to inflammation and kidney injury.

Key mediators of the production of Gd-IgA1 and its corresponding autoantibodies are B-cell activating factor (BAFF), and A proliferation-inducing ligand (APRIL), each playing essential roles in the survival and maintenance of B cells and humoral immunity. Elevated serum levels of both BAFF and APRIL are observed in patients with IgAN and correlate with disease severity.

There are already four approved therapies to treat IgAn — Novartis' Fabhalta — $129m revenues in 2024, Calliditas Therapeutics Tarpeyo — ~$150m revenues, and Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) Filspari — ~$124m revenues last year. Novartis Atrasentan was approved last month. Is there room in the IgAN market for three more therapies?

Vera makes the point that atacicept could secure label expansions into markets such as lupus, and outside of nephrology, rheumatology and hematology, and as mentioned in my last post, analysts have speculated ~$1.25bn in peak sales for the drug.

Analysis — Investing Strategy Ahead Of Critical Data Readout

In a statement in Vera's Q1 2025 earnings press release from May 6th, CEO Marshall Fordyce states:

We are rapidly approaching a significant Vera milestone with the upcoming primary endpoint results from the pivotal atacicept ORIGIN 3 trial, and if successful, we expect this to enable a BLA submission to the FDA in the second half of this year, which may allow approval and commercial launch in 2026. If approved, we believe that atacicept has the potential to advance the standard of care in IgA nephropathy

Not many people seem to doubt that the study will be successful—Phase 2b results showed a 66% reduction in Gd-IgA1, a "useful diagnostic and prognostic marker in IgAN patients", apparently, 75% resolution of hematuria, 52% reduction in proteinuria, and stabilisation of eGFR (estimated glomerular filtration rate). The fact that there are at least three other BAFF or BAFF/APRIL drugs in development suggests this MoA can work.

I would not have too many fears around the Phase 3 study data supporting an approval push, however if data suggests atacicept cannot challenge for standard of care, I would expect heavy share price losses, as there was in January 2023 when proteinuria reduction data disappointed the market and Vera's share price sank to ~$5.5 per share.

Vera reported a net loss of $52m in Q1, but also reported $590m of cash, so an approval push will not be an issue, although, to expand into new markets, more expensive studies will need to be funded, and in my experience, biotechs with newly commercialised drugs rarely make money—commercial launches are expensive, and it can take years to achieve full market penetration.

Vera's current market cap valuation is $1.35bn, and as mentioned, there are no other assets for the company to fall back on if atacicept is not a commercial success.

It's tempting to make the bull case that a positive data readout will send shares spiking back to their former value of >$45 per share, and having failed to call this spike last time around, would it be foolish to ignore it again?

I am not so sure—the competition is so intense, and in my view, only "best-in-class" Phase 3 data will do, with any other type of readout possibly resulting in a sell-off. Even if there is a spike, it will be over one year before atacicept hits the market, and it will have to eat the lunch of Novartis, Otsuka, Travere et al. in order to justify a multi-billion market-cap valuation.

Then again, we may note that Vertex paid $4.9bn to acquire Alpine Immune and its kidney disease therapy povetacicept last year, so what price might Vera fetch if a Big Pharma looks to acquire a derisked drug with “blockbuster” (>$1bn per annum) revenue potential, to target the iGAN market.

As readers may be able to tell, I am torn on the bull/bear thesis, and therefore, once again, I am offering a "Hold" recommendation.

It would be brave to buy stock ahead of the Phase 3 readout - presumably due within the next month—but there is a chance of doubling your money.

There is also a chance there is no spike, or potentially a sell-off, a chance of a steady erosion in value as the market concludes atacicpet / Vera does not have what it takes to compete in such a competitive market, or, alternatively, a chance a Big Pharma company pays $3bn—$5bn to take a likely "blockbuster" into battle in the IgAN markets.

data.title: Birkenstock Q2: Strong Performance Negates Tariff Worries
data.description:
Introduction

Birkenstock Holding plc (NYSE:BIRK) recently reported Q2 numbers, which beat estimates on top and bottom lines, as well as raising guidance for the rest of the year. It seems that the company’s brand is very strong, and no tariffs may affect its performance at all. I initiate with a Hold rating for now, and will revisit the company at a later stage, as its future seems very bright.

By The Numbers

Looking at the top line, sales came in at €574.3m, which is a 19% y/y increase, beating analysts’ estimates by €8m. Q2 GAAP EPS came in at €0.56, which also beat analysts’ estimates by a few cents. Looking at the revenue breakdown in a bit more detail, B2B remains the top revenue generator, with €432m in sales, while DTC came in at €140.75m. Both showed 19% growth y/y. In terms of revenue by region, the Americas and EMEA lead in total sales; however, APAC saw growth of 30% y/y, supported by new store openings, increasing the company’s brand presence. The Americas and EMEA saw growth of 23% and 12%, respectively, which are very good, too.

Looking at the company’s profitability and efficiency, BIRK’s gross margin for Q2 was around 58% vs. 56% last year, operating margins were 30.5% vs. 27.5% last year. So, a decent improvement across the board. The improvement was driven by pricing actions, better absorption of new manufacturing capacity, and some favorable currency effects. The company has said that its close-toe shoes outperformed sandals, which demand a higher average selling price (ASP), helping margins to improve y/y. Adjusted EBITDA margin grew to 34.8%, which shows that the company’s premiumization strategy is solid.

On the company’s financial position, BIRK ended the quarter with €235.4m in cash and equivalents, against €1.17B in long-term debt. Is this worrisome? Well, from the first glance at the company’s operating margins, I’m thinking not at all. But to be a bit more diligent, in Q2, the company paid €25.6m in interest expense, while its EBIT was €175.3m, which means the company’s interest coverage ratio is around 7x. This is well above what analysts consider to be safe (2x) and even above my more conservative requirement of at least 5x. Therefore, I don’t see any liquidity or solvency issues.

On top of these results, the company went ahead and updated its previous guidance for 2025. Revenue is expected to be at the high end of 15%-17% in constant currency. Adjusted EBITDA margin in the range of 31.3%-31.8%, up from the previous range of 30.8%-31.3%, so a 50bps increase. This implies an adjusted EBITDA range of €660-€670.

Overall, I think this was a decent quarter for the company. There were improvements on the top and bottom lines, as well as decent margin improvements. On top of that, an upgraded guidance was received very well by the investing community, sending the company’s share price up over 6% as of writing this. The company seems to be firing on all cylinders and is able to market its quality products very effectively, navigating the current uncertainty quite well.

Comments on the Outlook

So, speaking of current uncertainty, it is no surprise that the company will see some impact of tariffs on its operations. And of course, it is not a surprise that it will do whatever it can to pass on those costs to the consumer. No company is going to willingly absorb the increase in costs if it can easily pass it on to the customer. Especially not Birkenstock, which has a lot of pricing power due to the company’s strong brand equity and demand. It can easily pass any costs, whether that is from tariffs or inflation, without seeing a decline in demand.

The CEO, Oliver Reichert, is aware of how loyal the company’s customers are, saying that the customers are “very loose with our pricing power,” which gives the company enough room to further increase prices. So, the current tariff situation for Birkenstock can be seen as an opportunity and not a disadvantage, as it may have been for many other companies out there. The product the company has developed over the years, and the brand itself, are seen as high quality, with not many similar products to match the quality of a Birkenstock.

Another positive I see going forward is the company’s focus on the Direct-to-Consumer expansion. I have been a proponent of such an expansion when it comes to retail companies. Operating your physical stores as well as stable, easy-to-use online channels is a sure way of improving margins in the long run. So, I like that the company is turning its focus on DTC channels, which include physical stores and online. DTC has already outpaced B2B growth this quarter and is seen to be accelerating going forward.

Back in 2022, the company’s DTC sales consisted of 89% from the online store. This most likely has changed quite a bit, given that since then, the company has opened 31 new physical stores. It would be good to see what the breakdown is right now, but the company doesn’t seem to reveal such numbers any longer. It is worth noting that the management said the shift in channel mix is evident, as the younger generations are returning to shopping centers, boosting physical store sales. I would like to see further expansion in APAC as well as Latin America to further drive growth.

Closing Comments

I see the company will continue to perform quite well given its brand appeal and strong pricing power, but am I going to start a position right now? Unfortunately, no. It seems the company’s share price is a bit overextended right now, and essentially fully recovered from the drop during the beginning of the tariff circus.

Nevertheless, I am quite certain I will be adding the company to my portfolio over the next few quarters. I don’t think it will be trading at a much lower P/E than right now. I think it will be one of those companies like Costco (COST) that will perpetually seem expensive. These types of companies have a certain appeal and a following that will not let the share price become very cheap, and I’m fine with it. I’ll revisit the company in the next quarter to see how it has developed. It is added to my “potential buys” watchlist.

data.title: The Hershey Company: The Outlook Is Sour (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

While The Hershey Company (NYSE:HSY) is known for their sweets, the outlook for the stock seems sour as of now. In the below analysis, it is determined that while Hershey is in a neutral trading range currently, the vast majority of indications show that the odds favor a bearish breakdown. For the fundamentals, Q1 results were concerning but full year guidance was reiterated. While revenue growth is expected to be "at least 2%" for 2025, it is still a notable deterioration from 2024's growth rate. As for the valuation, while the P/S makes the stock seem around fairly valued relative to historic growth figures, the massive premium to the Consumer Staples sector is highly unjustified. Therefore, I initiate Hershey at a sell rating.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Hershey stock was in an uptrend from early February to early March when things changed abruptly and a downtrend formed. That downtrend line was broken in early April and now the stock is not in much of a trend at all. It seems to have settled into a trading range between the low 160s and the low 170s.

Support

There are three relevant support levels in this chart. The nearest one is the bottom of the trading range at about 161 as support has been found there for the past month. The next support level is at about 155. This level was resistance from mid to late January while being support briefly in mid February. Lastly, there is support in the mid 140s as a major upside gap occurred there in early February.

Resistance

There are also three important resistance levels to discuss. The first is the top of the trading range at about 172 as it has been resistance a few times in the past month or so. Moving up, the 180 area is likely to be resistance as well since it represents an early March gap down. Lastly, the 187 level seems to be a significant one as a downside gap appeared there in December. It has already acted as resistance in March.

Takeaway

I would say that the chart for Hershey is more or less neutral currently. The uptrend was broken but the downtrend also ended and so the stock is in a trading range. Furthermore, there is a good balance of support and resistance and so the stock may continue its horizontal drift.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average [SMA]

The 50 day SMA has been below the 200 day SMA for the past six months to show sustained long term bearishness. The gap between the SMAs did close significantly from late February to late April to show slowing downward momentum. So far this month, the gap between them has been relatively stable to show that downward momentum is now steady. The stock is now back below both SMAs as a sign of weakness.

Exponential Moving Average [EMA]

The 9 day EMA is currently a very slight margin above the 21 day EMA to show that perhaps the near term is a bit positive. However, the separation between the EMAs has been overall small since mid April as the stock is in a trading range. Therefore, I believe there hasn't been any true signals in the EMAs for the past month.

Bollinger Bands

Like the EMAs, there hasn't been any strong signals in the Bollinger Bands due to the fact that the stock is range bound. Recently, the stock was at the upper band to show that perhaps the odds favored an upside breakout, but on the most recent trading day, the stock has broken back near the lower band to show that downside is more likely.

Takeaway

While the EMAs and the Bollinger Bands lack major signals, I believe the SMAs show a weak technical outlook for Hershey. The SMAs have been in a bearish state for an extended period, and the stabilization of the gap between them shows that downward momentum has stopped decelerating. Furthermore, the 50 day SMA may have contributed as resistance recently, and so the SMAs may weigh on the stock moving forward.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD had a bearish crossover with the signal line back in early to mid March to show that weakness was creeping into the stock. However, the MACD and its signal line has been nearly inseparable since early to mid April. This once again reflects the current neutrality of the stock and uncertainty on the outlook.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

There was negative divergence in the RSI at the stock's early March peak as the indicator made a lower high compared to back in late February. Investors should have sensed the risk of a bearish reversal. Currently, the RSI sits at a reading of 43.97 and so the bears are in control by a fair margin according to this indicator.

Stochastics

Despite the stock being in a trading range, both the %K and %D were recently in the 80+ zone to show strong bulls. However, a bearish crossover occurred and now both lines are plunging to show weakness. Both lines remain above 50 but that may not last for long as the bears are expected to regain control in the near future.

Takeaway

A sense of neutrality was present in the indicators as well but I would say that they show net bearishness. The MACD showed uncertainty but the RSI's below 50 reading shows that the bears are in control currently. Furthermore, the stochastics just had a bearish crossover and both lines are plunging. As a result, the indicators show that perhaps downside is now a bit more likely.

Fibonacci Retracement

Using the early March high and the early April low, near term Fibonacci Retracement levels are provided above. It is clear that the 38.2% retracement has been the top of the trading range for Hershey, as the stock has failed to rally over that level. The 23.6% retracement hasn't held as support in the past month, and the stock is currently below that level. With the stock failing to even get near retracing 50% of the down move, the odds are great that the stock will break down from the trading range and resume its downtrend. This is in agreement with the analyses of the moving averages and indicators.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Hershey reported their 2025 Q1 earnings on May 1st and showed concerning results overall. The company generated consolidated net sales of $2.8054 billion, down 13.8% from the prior year period. This was still able to beat estimates by $11.95 million and so expectations were very conservative to begin with. As shown above, revenue growth has dropped to quite near its multiyear low and is far below the average of the past few years. Also shown above is their operating cash flow growth. The major QoQ deterioration to also near multiyear lows in this figure could be a cause for concern regarding the financial performance of the business. As for EPS, Hershey reported an adjusted EPS of $2.09, down 31.9% from the year ago quarter. This also surpassed analyst estimates as they beat by $0.16. Again, expectations were low. As a whole, there is not much to like in these earnings in my view as key metrics show a worrying direction for the company.

Full Year Guidance

Hershey reiterated their full year 2025 guidance as expectations were unchanged from their Q4 report. They are projecting net sales growth of at least 2% and so things are expected to improve after a weak Q1. Still it would be a considerable growth slowdown from full year 2024 growth of 8.7%. As for adjusted EPS, they are expecting declines in the mid 30s percentage range. Therefore, further weakness is likely for EPS with Q1's declines only being 31.9% YoY. This is also a major worsening from 2024's +33.2% growth rate for the figure. Overall, while I believe there is nothing to get excited about from these guidance figures, at least revenue growth is still expected to be positive for the full year and so better business activity may be seen in the next few quarters.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has seen significant contraction since its three year high set in early 2023. After plunging towards the 3.0 level earlier this year, the multiple has recovered to a reading of 3.705. I believe this potentially shows fair valuation relative to the company's growth history. As discussed above, while Q1 results show that revenues dropped 13.8% YoY, full year guidance of at least 2% revenue growth indicates that things may improve in the upcoming quarters. Therefore, the P/S ratio's rebound from three year lows has been justified. However, when compared to peers, I believe Hershey is clearly overvalued. The Consumer Staples sector has a median P/S ratio of only 1.19 as of this writing and so the stock is at a 211% premium according to this measure. Even though the upcoming quarters may be better, a full year growth rate of "at least 2%" is not nearly enough to justify such a valuation gap. Therefore, I conclude that Hershey is overvalued at current levels.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I determined that the technicals indicate a breakdown from the trading range is much more likely than an upside breakout. Signs in the SMAs, RSI, stochastics, and Fibonacci Retracement show that further downside is probable. For the fundamentals, Q1 earnings were weak and while guidance shows that revenue growth is expected to be positive for 2025, results are expected to be a disappointment relative to 2024. For the valuation, as discussed above, the P/S ratio shows that the stock may be fairly valued relative to past data but the major unjustified premium to peers leads me to conclude that the stock is overvalued. As a result, I initiate Hershey at a sell rating on the basis of technical downside potential and an undesirable fundamental setup.

data.title: Newegg: GMV Declines And A Major Lack Of Clarity
data.description:
The sharp recent rebound in the stock markets has brought many struggling companies back to life, including Newegg Commerce, Inc. (NASDAQ:NEGG), the electronics e-commerce website. Newegg was on the brink of being delisted from the NASDAQ before the company executed a 20:1 split to stay above listing requirements.

Recently in May, Newegg shares have nearly doubled on little news, though the company's recent Q4 earnings print showcased a complete lack of clarity on the year ahead. The question for investors now: is this small-cap stock a good value buy, or is it enjoying a phantom rally that will quickly subside?

In my view, it's the latter: I don't think Newegg has the fundamentals to compete very long in a crowded marketplace that is additionally dealing with fierce macroeconomic headwinds. I'm initiating the stock with a sell rating.

A storefront dedicated to the tech-savvy

First, let's get grounded on what Newegg does. The company has operated a popular e-commerce storefront specializing in computers and other tech equipment since 2001. The snapshot below showcases the company's home page:

Newegg's core differentiating factor is that it markets heavily toward tech-savvy, do-it-yourself buyers. The company's website offers a “PC Builder” where customers can assemble their own machines and customize parts. The company also has a huge presence in the gaming space, offering everything from gaming laptops with high-end graphics chips to gaming systems and video games.

The company is mostly a first-party retailer, with Best Buy (BBY) as its closest peer and its major direct competitor. However, like Amazon (AMZN), Newegg also operates a third-party e-commerce marketplace. The company has distribution deals with a number of major tech companies, including the likes of Samsung, Seagate (STX), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Nvidia (NVDA), to sell their consumer-facing products directly to Newegg shoppers, wherein Newegg earns a small commission.

The company believes the electronics e-commerce market will grow to a ~$1.3 trillion annual scale by 2028, indicating that the company's current ~$1.5 billion GMV is roughly only 0.1% penetrated into this overall market.

We note that Newegg is dwarfed by its competition. Best Buy, for example, currently runs at ~$14 billion in revenue per quarter. To me, there is very little room for Newegg to expand its appeal. A truly committed, tech-savvy, and DIY shopper may be able to save a small percentage off the price of a new computer by purchasing parts on Newegg instead of a plug-and-play, out-of-the-box laptop from Best Buy. While this market will likely always be present, Newegg's declining GMV (at a much faster rate than its peers) suggests that this niche is shrinking.

GMV declines and a lack of clarity for 2025

In April, Newegg released financial results for the quarter and year ending in December 2024, and it's not a pretty picture. As shown in the chart below, Newegg's revenue fell -18% y/y to $1.24 billion. Gross margins of 10.6%, meanwhile, also fell -60bps y/y, further aggravating the situation that reduced Newegg to a penny stock that needed a reverse split in the first place.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's GMV or gross merchandise volume, the total value of all sales on its platform, including the ~20% from third-party marketplace sales. Total GMV fell -15% y/y to $1.53 billion, with third-party marketplace GMV falling -14% y/y.

There are a couple of observations that are necessary to make here. Again, we note that Newegg's GMV is roughly a tenth of Best Buy's ~$14 billion in quarterly revenue. We should note as well that Newegg's declines stand in sharp contrast to its core rival.

Best Buy has also disappointed on comparable sales growth (revenue growth from existing stores, excluding newly opened and newly closed locations)—but that's mostly driven by the company's appliances division, like refrigerators and washers. Meanwhile, Best Buy's computing and mobile phones segment (the closest segment to Newegg's business mix) saw comparable sales grow at 6.5% y/y, while its consumer electronics division fell only -2% y/y.

Both Newegg and Best Buy face a weak consumer spending environment, and many consumers are delaying big-ticket upgrades on phones and computers. But it's evident that Newegg is suffering disproportionately with much sharper declines than its core rival.

We also should note that Newegg likes to think of itself not as a retailer like Best Buy, but as a vertical e-commerce provider that is top of mind in the tech category, the same as what Wayfair (W) is to furniture and Chewy (CHWY) is for pet supplies.

And yet, there's a major scale difference here as well. Chewy hit ~$12 billion in sales in 2024, growing in the mid single digits. Wayfair was also at roughly ~$12 billion in revenue, with roughly flat growth trends. Newegg, meanwhile, is at a tenth of that scale and declining. As a merchant with a logistics platform, Newegg desperately needs economies of scale that it doesn't have, which makes it nearly impossible for Newegg to reach the success of these other vertically integrated peers.

Can Newegg swing to profitability and entice a buyout?

Now, where Newegg has been successful recently is in shrinking its adjusted EBITDA losses. In FY24, as shown below, the company's adjusted EBITDA clocked in at -$9.5 million or a -0.1% margin, less than half of the prior-year loss.

The company improved adjusted EBITDA despite top-line headwinds and a 60bps reduction in gross margin, mainly by cutting SG&A spending by 23% y/y, a faster drop than its revenue declines. The company has worked hard to reduce its inventory, shed headcount, and shrink its real estate footprint in order to produce better profitability metrics.

The core hope and “upside risk” for Newegg is to keep ticking up its adjusted EBITDA toward a meaningful level of profitability, and then market itself to a private equity buyer. To me, however, the key obstacle to doing this is Newegg's ability to grow its third-party marketplace sales—which currently are declining at a double-digit pace. The company is already targeting a reduction in inventory: if it can convince more third-party partners to set up storefronts on the Newegg site, it will boost its high-margin commission stream, potentially to a point of hitting positive adjusted EBITDA and making a compelling case for a private equity buyout.

That being said, this possibility is very limited amid major uncertainties for 2025. We note that in the company's most recent earnings release, it declined to provide any guidance figures for FY25, noting only as follows:

However, a key challenge for the remainder of 2025 will be the impact of tariff policy on consumer confidence and purchasing behavior. As a result of this uncertainty, we are not prepared to deliver full year 2025 guidance at this time. As of December 31, 2024, our $50 million credit facility remained fully undrawn. Additionally, we effected a twenty-to-one share combination on April 7, 2025 in order to regain compliance with Nasdaq’s minimum bid price requirement, which we achieved on April 22, 2025."

The company made this announcement on April 28—after the close of its (as yet unreported) first quarter, and it presumably already has sales data for the first quarter. My read on this lack of clarity is that if Newegg had good news to report, it would have initiated guidance alongside its effort to shore up its stock.

Valuation and key takeaways

In my view, Newegg is going to keep disappointing investors in 2025, especially after its sharp recent rally that has very little basis in fundamentals.

The only thing that Newegg has going for it is a very low valuation, and a significant amount of net cash on its books. At current share prices near $7, Newegg trades at a $150.7 million market cap; netting off the $96.2 million of cash and $7.1 million of debt on Newegg's latest balance sheet gives us a $54.2 million enterprise value.

If we assume a ~10% revenue decline in FY25 to ~$1.11 billion in revenue (in line with recent double-digit y/y decline trends), Newegg trades at a ~0.05x forward revenue multiple. Best Buy, meanwhile, trades at ~0.4x revenue, as shown in the chart below.

Still, given Newegg's poor sales trends and its recent losses, on top of the harsh reality of the macroeconomy plus a very competitive retail field, it's more likely that Newegg will burn through its remaining cash reserves as it struggles to maintain its relevance in the marketplace.

In short: don't believe the recent phantom rally. In my view, Newegg stock will go back to capitulating downward very shortly.

data.title: Rockets Return To Earth, But Rocket Lab Likely Won't Return To Under $20
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Rocket Lab USA, Inc. (NASDAQ:RKLB) back in April, when we had just learnt the big news of a potential new government contract on the table. A whopping $5.6 billion U.S. Space Force contract, and investors are hoping this will be the needed boost for Rocket Lab. Of course, the securing of such a contract will require a successful Neutron launch. It's no secret that this will be the catalyst for Rocket Lab; a triumphant launch will send the stock through the roof.

Today I'm going to give you everything we learnt about the upcoming Neutron launch, the latest developments which point towards them hitting that 2H 2025 debut date. I'll also give a quick rundown of earnings, and a few things I picked up on the call make me particularly bullish on this company.

I've actually been bullish on them for quite some time, and have been following the stock personally since it was $7 last year. I think now is a good time to double down on Rocket Lab, and 2025 still has a lot to offer to investors. I'm reiterating my buy call.

Q1 2025 Rundown

Going into earnings, Wall Street was betting on Rocket Lab making a tidy revenue sum of $121.37 million and an EPS estimate of -$0.09. Historically, they’ve beaten revenue earnings 50% of the time, so anyone who bought in before this quarterly update was gambling. If you remember, management had given this quarter a guidance of between $117 and $123 million.

1Q25 revenue came in nicely at $122.7M, with about $87 million from space systems and $37 million from launches. After the results came in, the stock dropped 12%. That was due to the company losing $0.12 per share, more than the forecasted $0.10 loss. The next day, I bought back in at $20.55, and I'm very happy with that. Investors should be happy with that, too. I wouldn't expect this stock to drop back into the $16-17 range.

Upcoming Launch for Anyone Wanting to Watch

Next on the schedule for Rocket Lab is a satellite launch for the Institute for Q-shu Pioneers of Space, Inc. (iQPS). This is a repeat customer for Rocket Lab, and it is sending another big satellite into orbit on May 17th. The launch will be a routine Electron launch, bagging Rocket Lab another $7 million paycheck.

Successful Electron launches have become so expected from Rocket Lab now that the stock doesn't react when the rocket boosts into orbit without blowing up. What people really want to see is more confirmation around the Neutron launch date. I'm here to tell you the Neutron is getting closer, I can almost smell the rocket fuel, it's that close. Peter Beck on the latest call mentioned the:

“biggest news item in this quarter has been the Pentagon's high value launch contract with the National Security Space Launch Program (NSSL).”

This contract is front and center to Rocket Lab management; it's a potential $5.6 billion, to put this in context, that's almost 50 times their quarterly revenue. What makes me really optimistic is that there is the possibility that this contract can be split. That is, we won't see the total sum go to SpaceX (SPACE); Rocket Lab may get a good chunk of the pie. This would also make it the only publicly traded company to ever onboard the NSSL.

So here's the checklist Rocket Lab need to hit. Neutron has to have a successful first launch, that's why they're being so careful around the launch date, they’re taking every precaution to make sure this rocket takes off. After that, they'll be bidding for task orders under the Phase 2 Lane 1 program, which means they’re free to throw their hat in the ring for the NSSL contract. They are allowed to bid for this anytime until June 2029. You might then think we have loads of time to get Neutron off the ground, but remember, we’re not the only contenders in this race; SpaceX already has a workhorse rocket. The good news is they’re already having talks with the big dogs, they’ve completed a kickoff meeting with all the key government agencies they’d be working with, the US Space Force, the National Reconnaissance Office, and the Office of Space Launch. This kick-off meeting is part of the $5 million “pre-contract” deal given to them, a small test to show them they're real players in this game.

Why the Stock Blew Up 10% Yesterday

If you're following Rocket Lab closely, you would have noticed a 10% bump yesterday. It brought the stock up to $24, and since then, it hasn't dropped much; it's showing some resistance at $23. I believe this is the new norm for the stock; it's not going under $20 anytime soon.

So why the 10% boost? Well, a while ago, Rocket Lab supported a mission for a company called Varda, they used an Electron to loft a W-3 space capsule into orbit. Now, what was different about this launch was that it was a return to Earth mission. They had to send it into orbit and let it come down safely after some time. These kinds of missions are a big deal, a lot of companies a looking to test things in space under zero gravity conditions.

Take Varda Space Industries, Inc., a pharmaceutical company, I believe they were looking into testing how a new drug compound reacts under space conditions, interesting stuff right. This is the second time in 2 months they’ve successfully done this type of mission, which opens them up to completely new customers. Companies that need their satellites in space know Rocket Lab can deliver; now, companies that just want their stuff in orbit for short periods of time can depend on Rocket Lab as well.

Valuation

Rocket Lab, even at $23, is still grossly undervalued in my opinion. Yes, it has a relatively small market cap at $10 billion compared to some of its competition, like SpaceX, but Rocket Lab is growing faster than any of them. The RSI looks attractive right now at 59 as it's on its way down. I'm also here to tell you not to listen to Seeking Alpha’s valuation; they give it a forward price-to-sales ratio of 19.56 against a sector median of 1.48. Likewise, their forward EV to sales is 19.52 against a median of 1.97.

This crazy valuation is only due to the stockpiling of all space tech companies into one basket. Of course, you are going to get smaller companies in there that pull down that median. In reality, very few are doing what Rocket Lab is right now, designing a rocket that pulls 13 tonnes up into LEO and still comes down ready to be reused.

What's Next

Expect several return-to-Earth mission contracts coming in for Rocket Lab this year. They are essentially an Electron launch, but with a different cargo. This news opens up their market to new customers. In terms of the Neutron, we're still on track to hit that 2H 2025 launch date.

Rocket Lab USA, Inc. is showing me all the right signs. I'm still fully behind this stock, I'm reiterating my Buy rating, soon this stock should be trading at quite a premium.

data.title: CGCV: Lower Volatility And Better Resilience Than The S&P 500
data.description:
Overview

Capital Group Conservative Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:CGCV) is a newer ETF that focuses on companies that may offer superior resilience through market declines. The fund is relatively new, with an inception dating back to June of 2024. CGCV has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.33% and is managed by Capital Group. With markets seeing such a high level of volatility and uncertainty, an ETF that offers less volatility and better resilience certainly earns its place in a diversified portfolio. I like the overall objective of the fund as it can serve as a solid alternative for investors that cannot stomach higher volatility in the market, but still want to maintain exposure to a diverse range of equities.

Looking at the performance of CGCV since its inception, we can see that the total return sits a little above 11%. Although CGCV also saw its share price decline in response to the ongoing trade wars, the price recovered much faster than the S&P 500's (SPX) price. Even though CGCV's price also declined, we can also see that the drop was a lot less dramatic than SPX. So at first glance, CGCV achieves what it sets out to do by offering better downside resilience and total returns during this period of inflated uncertainty.

While CGCV aims to offer a better sense of security and capital preservation, investors must be aware of the tradeoffs involved with an ETF like this. While there are instances that prove the efficiency of CGCV's strategy, the choice to focus on less volatility and better resilience has the potential to lead to lower total returns over a longer period of time. In order to achieve better resilience, CGCV excludes exposure to some of the popular large-cap companies that are known to provide rapid earnings growth. With the size of the AI market estimated to rapidly increase over the next decade, CGCV could fall behind. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, CGCV has net assets of approximately $336.8M that is spread across 91 different positions. Surprisingly, CGCV maintains a sizeable exposure to the Information Technology sector, which is typically known to be more volatile. Information Technology currently has a weight of 17.7% of assets. This is followed by exposure to investments within Health Care and Industrials, accounting for 15.1% and 14.9% of assets respectively. The combination of these three assets makes up approximately 47.7% of assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on CGCV's daily price movement and total return potential.

Tech is arguably the most rapidly changing sector there and valuations are often inflated as there is a ton of optimism around the sector. However, CGCV specifically focuses on Technology holdings that pay a dividend, which usually include stocks with a steadier price. This is why Microsoft (MSFT) is the largest holding at 6.2% of assets. This is followed by varying weights to other high quality large-cap companies like RTX Corp (RTX), Apple (AAPL), AbbVie (ABBV), and General Electric (GE) just to name a few.

I believe that CGCV has an excellent mix of holdings, and many of these businesses share some of the same underlying strengths. For instance, CGCV avoids companies that have inflated price to earnings ratios or companies that have a lot of speculation around their potential rather than their current results.

Since CGCV only focuses on companies that pay dividends, the ETF will avoid rapid moving companies like Netflix (NFLX) or Amazon (AMZN). A lot of the holdings have an established history of raising dividends, which is an indication of a business that has consistent cash flows through different market cycles. Here are some notable characteristics of some holdings within CGCV:

  • Broadcom (AVGO): Has increased dividends for 14 consecutive years without disruption, while averaging a five year dividend CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 13.63%.
  • Meta Platforms (META): Only recently started paying a dividend back in early 2024 but has over $28B in cash and equivalents, indicating there is a potential for plenty of continued growth through buybacks and capex.
  • AbbVie (ABBV): Recently reported strong Q1 earnings, beating expectations by $0.30. The business also raised guidance for 2025, indicating growing revenue streams for the year.

CGCV Actually Offers Better Resilience

Since the fund's lifespan is still so short, we don't really have a large operating window to reference. However, we can see that CGCV's strategy has proven effective during the recent market decline. Markets sank at the tail end of Q1 due to the uncertainty around President Trump's tariff proposals. Now that it seems like the US and China have come to an agreement, markets are finally rallying again.

Looking at a quick comparison below, we can see that CGCV is outperforming a traditional index-tracking ETF like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) on a YTD basis. CGCV didn't see its price decline as severely during the sharpest drop, proving the effectiveness of its strategy. Additionally, CGCV's price was able to participate in the market recovery in recent weeks.

Since CGCV's operating history is so short, I thought it would be a good idea to compare the price movements of the underlying holdings of SPY to see how they compare. As we can see, the majority of the top holdings have actually experienced sizeable growth on a YTD basis. The exceptions here are Apple (AAPL) and UnitedHealth (UNH), which are both underperforming SPY by a wide margin. Apple has been impacted by fear around how tariffs will impact its business and growing competition in the Artificial Intelligence space. UNH suffered from a dramatic price decline due to the CEO stepping down because of 'personal reasons'.

We still don't know what the actual ripple effects of the new tariff policies will be six months from now, so a fund like CGCV could be a great defensive position to navigate the uncertainty. We also have other sources of risk and uncertainty in the market that can add to the appeal of CGCV. For instance, the outlook around interest rate cuts still remains questionable as the Fed kept rates unchanged during their latest meeting. There's still a risk of inflation remaining higher than anticipated and an upward trending unemployment rate that can slow economic growth and cause a shift in consumer spending habits.

Dividend Potential

The dividend history is relatively short but the latest declared quarterly payout was $0.10 per share. Assuming the same payout for the next three quarters, the estimated annual dividend yield sits around 1.4%. While this yield isn't strong enough to compete with some of the popular dividend focused ETFs out there, I believe that the underlying strategy that CGCV implements can lead to this ETF offering a competitive dividend growth rate. CGCV puts an emphasis on companies that have a history of paying out consistent dividends and have healthy balance sheets.

These are businesses that may have established a reputation for their consistent dividend payouts. A strong balance sheet may also indicate that these businesses have a growing free cash flow and a relatively low payout ratio, which increases the odds of dividend raises. Although the history of the fund is short, here are some notable dividend traits for some of the holdings that I believe can eventually translate to dividend growth for CGCV.

  • RTX (RTX) has increased its dividend for over 31 consecutive years without disruption.
  • JPMorgan (JPM) has an impressively low dividend payout ratio of 24.79%, and a ten year dividend CAGR of 12.18%.
  • Coca-Cola (KO) earned the reputation of a dividend king by consistently raising dividends for over 62 years.

Vulnerability

It's common knowledge that technology companies typically choose to reinvest their earnings back into their business as a way to compound growth. Reinvesting back into the business is typically a better use of capital and this is why we see that many of the largest companies in the world don't pay a dividend. If they do pay a dividend, it's usually very insignificant compared to the price growth experienced. For instance, companies like Tesla (TSLA) don't pay a dividend and a company like Nvidia (NVDA) does pay a dividend but it's very small.

The biggest driver of growth in the Technology sector is the new innovations and products focused around Artificial Intelligence. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR of 19.20% through 2034! As companies continue to allocate capital towards AI investments, there's a good chance that we continue to see valuations across the sector go higher.

Even though CGCV does have exposure to some of the companies that are participating in AI endeavors, CGCV could underperform more traditional ETFs that track the Nasdaq. We can see below that CGCV has again demonstrated the effectiveness of its strategy by outperforming Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) through all of the recent market uncertainty. However, I anticipate that CGCV could underperform an ETF like QQQ over a much longer time frame. So investors may be giving up the best total return by choosing a fund like CGCV that prioritizes less volatility.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate CGCV as a buy due to the proven efficiency of its strategy. The ETF has outperformed both SPY and QQQ since its inception. While this may change at some point in the future, CGCV has already proven that it can offer better resilience and less volatility than index-tracking ETFs. The fund chooses to maintain exposure to dividend-paying companies that have a healthy balance sheet. I believe that this can lead to CGCV offering a decent rate of dividend growth over time, despite the smaller starting dividend yield. With plenty of sources of uncertainty in the market, CGCV is a compelling buy for the remainder of 2025.

data.title: Cisco Q3 2025: The Market Still Doesn't Get It
data.description:
Introduction

Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO) is finally being rewarded for years of hard work, product innovation, smart partnerships, and balance sheet discipline. Fiscal Q3 results came in strong across the board, with revenue and earnings above the high end of guidance. AI infrastructure orders hit $600M for the quarter and over $1B year to date [YTD], a full quarter ahead of Cisco's target.

And yet, Cisco still trades at a forward P/E under 17 and a PEG above 3.0, despite outperforming Arista (ANET), Juniper (JNPR), and most other networking peers on a 1-year basis. I believe the stock remains undervalued given its upside exposure to enterprise AI, sovereign cloud buildouts, and the improving trajectory of its core business. I'm rating CSCO a Buy for cautious investors who understand that this stock, most likely, won’t skyrocket. It’s a slow and steady AI compounder with value protection and optionality.

Quarter Highlights

Revenue came in at $14.15B (+11.4% Y/Y), beating expectations by about $91 million. Non-GAAP EPS of $0.96 beat by $0.04, while GAAP EPS of $0.62 was in line.

The company’s gross margin (non-GAAP) expanded to 68.6%, and Cisco generated $4.1B in operating cash flow. Product revenue grew 15%, while services rose 3%.

Importantly, Cisco is seeing clear traction in AI, particularly with hyperscalers. In Q3 alone, webscale AI infrastructure orders surpassed $600M, bringing the FY total to over $1B, already above their full-year target. About two-thirds of those orders were systems (not just optics), meaning Cisco’s Silicon One is now competing toe-to-toe with Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) in core AI networking architectures. Triple-digit growth from 3 of the top 6 hyperscalers confirms Cisco’s relevance in the new compute stack.

Chuck Robbins (the CEO of Cisco) was visibly confident this quarter, and for good reason. Cisco's momentum is not just about AI: total product orders grew 20% Y/Y (+9% excluding Splunk), recurring revenue grew 5%, subscription revenue now makes up 56% of total revenue, and ARR sits at $30.6B.

On May 15, Cisco announced that it will pay its usual $0.41 per share quarterly dividend, with a payment date set for July 23 and an ex-dividend date of July 3. This puts the forward yield at roughly 2.57%, which might not sound flashy, but in this context, it’s actually quite attractive. While some AI names are all hype and zero cash flow, Cisco is returning real money to shareholders every quarter and still has over $15 billion allocated for buybacks.

Valuation Still Reflects Yesterday’s Cisco

Despite all this progress, CSCO continues to trade like a legacy hardware vendor. It trades at just 16.21x forward non-GAAP earnings, compared to 38.5x for Arista, 35x for Broadcom, and 17.7x for Juniper. Even its EV/EBITDA (TTM) multiple of 17.65x is significantly below ANET's 36.15x and AVGO's 27.74x.

I saw the argument that this discount could be justified by Cisco's weaker growth. Revenue declined 5.34% Y/Y, and EBITDA fell 17.43%, both considerably worse than peers. However, this backward-looking data misses the core inflection that is happening now. Cisco's AI revenue is non-linear, recurring business is just getting started, and sovereign deals (like Saudi Arabia's HUMAIN) haven’t even been included in the company's books yet. It’s clear that investors are already re-rating Cisco, but not nearly to the extent of its AI-exposed peers. Although over the last 12 months, CSCO has outperformed ANET and JNPR in price return:

  • CSCO: +30.99%

  • ANET: +17.19%

  • JNPR: +4.68%

  • AVGO: +66.25%.

Why This Time Feels Different

What makes this AI cycle different for Cisco is that it’s deeply embedded in the architecture. For the first time in a decade, Cisco is involved in the technology stack that drives next-gen compute, not just the network around it. Its Silicon One chips are now integrated into Nvidia's Ethernet reference architecture. The upcoming sovereign cloud demand, starting with Saudi Arabia’s HUMAIN, could turn Cisco into a central AI infrastructure vendor for countries, not just companies. And in enterprise, Cisco is positioning itself as a turnkey provider of AI-ready infrastructure, where networking, compute, and security are working together.

Internally, the company is aligning itself for long-term growth. Leadership changes, including the promotion of Jeetu Patel and the addition of OpenAI's Kevin Weil to the board, signal a realignment around future-focused innovation. Besides, as I already mentioned, its AI momentum is powered by its partnerships with Nvidia, its involvement in the AI Infrastructure Partnership, and its integration with key hyperscalers, so it’s safe to assume the AI wave won’t go past Cisco. This time, it looks like the company had caught a wave.

Risks

Of course, as always, there are things to watch and to be cautious about. Cisco’s core growth metrics remain weak, with forward revenue and EBITDA growth projected below 3%. It’s not clear how long hyperscaler AI orders will sustain their current momentum, and whether enterprise adoption will ramp as quickly as bulls hope. Tariffs could pressure margins again, though management says worst-case assumptions are already baked into Q4 guidance.

Also, despite new security wins (up 54% Y/Y, helped by Splunk), the legacy firewall business could remain a liability. Investors should look for signs that platform adoption (SASE, XDR, Hypershield) continues to offset appliance declines.

Conclusion

Cisco is transitioning from a network equipment vendor into a full-stack AI infrastructure giant. Its valuation still reflects its past, not today’s performance. With hyperscaler orders already booming, sovereign deals underway, and new products tightly integrated with Nvidia’s reference architecture, I believe Cisco is on the verge of a multi-year re-rating. Meanwhile, investors are paid to wait with a 2.68% dividend and $15.6B in buyback firepower, which is not a bad deal at all.

This stock isn’t about strong momentum. But for investors looking for durable AI exposure, strong cash generation, and re-rating potential, it’s a Buy.

data.title: Main Street Capital: Not Worth The Premium
data.description:
Main St Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is a leading BDC which enjoys a premium reputation with investors. MAIN has been a strong performer for years and is priced at a large premium compared to BDC peers - it’s widely viewed as the King of BDCs.

MAIN is focused on the lower middle market segment, lending to companies with revenues between $10m-$150m and EBITDA of below $20m. This smaller ticket size enables MAIN to underwrite loans with a controlling share, and implement protective terms and covenants in loan agreements. The majority are 1st lien loans.

It also takes equity stakes in its borrowers.

However, in times of economic stress, smaller companies are more likely to encounter operating difficulties.

MAIN is internally managed. Internally managed REITS and BDCs often make better investments as management interests are generally better aligned, and costs kept lower.

Investor Returns

MAIN has rewarded shareholders well over the last decade, delivering a 277% total return, handily exceeding that of the S&P 500 (SPY).

More recently, however, MAIN share price has dipped. MAIN has underperformed SPY by around 7%, and also underperformed the BDC segment represented here by the VanEck ETF (BIZD) by around 5%.

The share price has been driven by a consistent long term performance of growth. NAV and Dividend per share have grown consistently, while distributable net income per share - DNII, has been dropping with recent rate reductions.

Valuation

All this comes with a premium valuation.

MAIN closing NAV was $32, at current prices of $53, this is a 65% premium to NAV.

As per the table below from BDC Investor, this is a significant premium to peers.

MAIN pays a monthly dividend of $.26, equating to an annual yield of 5.9%, however also pays regular special dividends, which have boosted the payout significantly in recent years.

While investors have become accustomed to the extra dividend payments, management clearly separates these, and keeps the flexibility to reduce or discontinue these.

Latest Earnings

MAIN just delivered Q1 earnings on May 8th.

Year on year for the quarter shows deterioration in several metrics.

While investment income has grown by 4%, interest expenses are up 16% and internal expenses up 6%, driving the DNII margin down 250bps from 71.7% to 69.2%.

Realised losses increased from $12.3m to $29.5m.

However MAIN recorded an unrealised gain increasing from $40m to $63m for the quarter. This drove up NAV from operations by 8%.

This translates to flat NII overall, but NAV per share was also down by 4%, due to the increase in the share count from 85.1m to 88.7m.

You can see the NAV walk here. Note how much the NAV appreciation relies on unrealised gains.

This is a tricky area, as there is little transparency into the valuation of the loans and especially equity investments. The companies are as a rule not publicly traded, so the valuation carry relies on MAIN management estimates.

It's worth watching how much the unrealised gains translate into actual gains quarter by quarter, which can be a check on how realistic the valuations are.

The LMM investment portfolio is about half of the total, carried at $2.6bn fair value. The equity ownership is 40% of that, so around $1bn in value. This $1bn equity portfolio cost around $750m, so around $250m of the total NAV of $5.2bn is unrealised gains on the equity portfolio, or around 5%.

Overall, MAIN carries its assets at an 18% increase on acquisition cost, which is a 3% increase in fair value year on year.

Funding Costs

MAIN has reasonably staggered debt maturities, however some near term maturities are worth noting. The $150m 2025 notes and the $500m 2026 notes were issues in 2022 and 2021 respectively, while rates were at a low point. The rollover of these is likely to adversely impact funding costs.

Rate Sensitivity

While the Fed has hit pause on reducing rates, the market is pricing 50-75 bps rate cuts in the coming year. Each 50 bps cut reduces NII per share by $.05

Insider Ownership

Management proudly presents the strong insider ownership, with 3.4 million shares worth close to $200m held by executives.

What is not disclosed is that, after years of no insider trades, $12m of net sales were made in 2 trades on 26th March, before the close of the quarter.

They weren't just any insiders, but David Magdol, the CIO, and Dwayne Hyzak, the CEO. Both dumped about a quarter of their holdings at the same price of $58. At $6m and $7m proceeds, I doubt they needed these sales to pay for groceries or school fees. This looks like a concerning lack of confidence in the prospects of the business.

Analysts

In general, analysts are neutral on MAIN, with an average Hold rating.

Wall St analysts target a flat price.

Short Interest

On 14th May Jehosaphat Research, a renowned short seller, published a short thesis on MAIN.

This argues that the MAIN share price is over valued on the back of inflated asset valuation, and hints at accounting issues.

Short interest is now building, and stands at 4%.

Summary And How I Will Play This

  • MAIN has had a great run, and investors have been rewarded handsomely.
  • From a valuation perspective, MAIN is an outlier, priced at a huge premium to book.
  • With a potential recession on the horizon, the business outlook for BDCs is deteriorating.
  • Business performance is deteriorating year on year.
  • NAV has a growing portion of unrealised gains, and assets are held at an average fair value of 18% over cost.
  • Upcoming debt maturities are likely to raise funding costs, which any rate cuts will reduce interest income.
  • A special dividend cut would put selling pressure on the stock from income investors.
  • Analysts expect no upside in the share price.
  • Technicals are weak.
  • Insider trades show management selling down significant stakes 10% higher than today's price.
  • If they are selling, so am I.

I am long MAIN at a cost base of $45, but sold calls against 80% of my position which was called in March. I will now exit the balance of my position and sell short puts against the proceeds. The December $40 puts offer a nice place to hide and watch this play out. At $1.60 premium this trade offers a double digit economic return with a large margin of safety.

data.title: Trading Crude Oil With The Leveraged UCO And SCO ETFs
data.description:
Nearby NYMEX crude oil futures (CL1:COM) reached their highest price since 2008 in March 2022 at $130.50 per barrel. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine lit a bullish fuse under the energy commodity. Over the past three years, the energy commodity has made lower highs and lower lows.

The last time the price was above $100 on the nearby NYMEX WTI futures was in July 2022. In 2023 and 2024, the oil futures market traded around the $80 per barrel level. The pivot point moved lower to the $70 level in late 2024.

After reaching a high of just over $80 per barrel in early January 2025, crude oil futures have been falling, eclipsing the $70 and $60 levels. The price dropped below a critical technical support level in April at the May 2023 $63.47 low. Crude oil has not sustained rallies over the past months, and the trend remains bearish in May 2025. The energy commodity has been trading around the $60 per barrel level since early April 2025.

Crude oil falls and trades around the $60 per barrel level

Nearby NYMEX crude oil prices for June 2025 delivery remained in a bearish trend throughout 2025.

The chart highlights the bearish pattern of lower highs and lower lows throughout 2025, with this year’s peak on the June futures contract at the January 15, 2025, high of $75.51 per barrel. While crude oil declined 27.6% at the April 9 low of $54.67, the energy commodity recovered to a $64.87 high on April 23, where it ran out of upside steam and fell back below $60 to a slightly higher $55.30 low on May 5. The most recent rally took the price to a lower $63.90 high on May 13, where crude oil futures appear to have turned lower.

The $60 level has become pivotal for crude oil in April and May, but the technical path of least resistance remains lower.

U.S. energy and OPEC policies could send crude oil lower than $50 per barrel

Oil fundamentals continue to favor the downside. Under the Trump administration, U.S. energy policy is removing regulations and encouraging producers to “drill-baby-drill.” The President believes falling oil prices will reduce inflationary pressures, leading to lower U.S. interest rates, which is critical for the administration’s economic agenda.

Meanwhile, OPEC+, the international oil cartel, and Russia, which cooperates with the cartel on production policy, increased output quotas in April. Moreover, OPEC+ agreed to boost production by an additional 411,000 barrels per day in June.

Even as output increases, the U.S. tariffs have increased the odds of a recession and the potential for stagflation. At the May 7 FOMC meeting, the U.S. central bank left short-term rates unchanged at the 4.375% level, citing rising stagflationary pressures caused by the U.S. tariffs.

The trade standoff between the U.S. and China is critical for the worldwide economy, as they are the world’s two leading economies. As the world’s leading consumer market, China relies on the U.S. for export revenues. The longer the trade issues continue, the greater the odds that the Chinese economy will suffer. Slower economic growth in China will weigh on global petroleum demand at a time when the international cartel and the United States are increasing output. Fundamentally, crude oil prices could be heading lower, with the technical trend validating the supply and demand factors.

Geopolitical factors have underpinned the leading energy commodity

While technical and fundamental factors support lower crude oil prices, the geopolitical landscape remains a potentially explosive issue for the energy commodity. The ongoing wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, U.S. relations with China, Iran, Russia, North Korea, and the newest conflict between India and Pakistan could cause rising global hostilities that impact crude oil production, refining, and logistics. Therefore, the potential for upside price spikes remains a clear and present danger for the oil futures market in May 2025.

Moreover, trade deals that boost the U.S. and Chinese economies could increase the energy demand.

The long-term trend suggests lower prices

Crude oil has made lower highs and lower lows since the 2022 peak.

The long-term quarterly chart since the early 1980s highlights that crude oil futures exploded to over $130 per barrel in 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine. Oil rose to its second-highest price in history, falling short of the 2008 $147.27 per barrel all-time high. Since Q1 2022, the energy commodity has declined, making lower highs, and falling to the most recent Q2 2025 $55.12 continuous contract low.

The monthly chart shows NYMEX crude oil’s pivot point has declined from $80 in 2022 through the first half of 2024, to $70 in the second half of 2024 through the first quarter of 2025, to $60 most recently. The trend is always a trader or investor’s best friend, and it remains lower in NYMEX crude oil futures in May 2025.

UCO and SCO are leveraged ETFs that track WTI crude oil prices

While the most direct avenues for long and short risk positions in the crude oil market are the CME’s NYMEX futures and futures options, the ProShares Ultra Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF product (NYSEARCA:UCO) and the ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF (SCO) product provide an alternative to the futures arena for market participants looking to take advantage of rising or falling petroleum prices.

At around $21 per share, UCO had over $412 million in assets under management. UCO trades an average of over 2.58 million shares daily and charges the same 0.95% management fee.

At around $20 per share, SCO had over $102.3 million in assets under management. SCO trades an average of over 1.33 million shares daily and charges a 0.95% management fee. The most recent significant decline in the June NYMEX crude oil futures market took the price 23.8% lower from $71.76 on April 2, 2025, to $54.67 per barrel on April 9, 2025.

Over the same period, the SCO ETF rose 48.4% from $16.52 to $24.52 per share. While SCO delivered twice the percentage decline, the ETF is only appropriate for short-term bearish risk positions.

The most recent rally in June NYMEX crude oil futures took the price 15.6% higher from $55.30 on May 5 to $63.90 per barrel on May 13.

Over the same period, the UCO ETF rose 22.6% from $18.35 to $22.50 per share. While UCO and SCO delivered performance that magnified the price action, the ETFs are only appropriate for short-term risk positions.

UCO and SCO’s leverage causes the ETFs to outperform when crude oil prices move in the desired direction, but they will underperform when prices move contrary to expectations or when petroleum prices remain stable as time decay erodes the leveraged ETFs’ value. Therefore, price and time stops are required when trading from the long or short side with the UCO and SCO ETFs.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this ETF. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as this. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

UCO’s Seeking Alpha ETF Grades highlight the F in momentum, and an F grade in risk. The nearly 1% management fee earns a D- in expenses, while the daily trading volume and assets under management receive an A in liquidity.

SCO’s Seeking Alpha ETF Grades highlight the D+ in momentum, and an F grade in risk. The nearly 1% management fee earns a D- in expenses, while the daily trading volume and assets under management receive an A- in liquidity.

UCO and SCO’s leverage makes them short-term trading tool for oil bulls, bears, and range traders. A potential drawback is that they only trade during U.S. stock market hours. Therefore, the ETFs can miss highs or lows when the stock market is not operating, as crude oil futures trade around the clock.

Crude oil remains in a bearish trend, with fundamentals supporting lower prices. SCO is a product that can add short exposure in portfolios for market participants expecting crude oil to challenge the $50 per barrel level soon. UCO is a tool for those looking for higher crude oil prices.
